item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really really like this. It's always neat to see the clinical writing standards of the Foundation flipped on their heads. The use of (beautiful) sensory language as the sole method of describing the skip was pleasant to read, and I'm intrigued by the hints of a larger narrative. I'm gonna be chewing on this one for a bit. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Toaster is only difficult to describe because I impose myself over the researcher's sense of self, resulting in a confusion of pronouns where I can only refer to myself in the first person singular.

055 is remarkable in that you can't remember what it is, but it's possible to remember what it isn't (for instance, it's not a sphere).

This one confuses the mind at the most basic levels; there's something about it that doesn't merely defy categorization, but goes on to defy the very concept of categories. If you try to think about it, the thoughts are bigger than you are. You can't grasp it, not because it's slippery like 055 or assertive like the Toaster, but because the human mind simply is not able to conceive of what it is. It's like trying to pick up a bowling ball with a pair of tweezers - the bowling ball is there, the tweezers can touch it, but they can't grasp it at all because it's just too big.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like the dissociation of Series V, albeit slightly more compelling. I don't like it one bit, but I don't hate it enough to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The prose is good, and has some great imagery, but I don't understand why this is an SCP rather than its own story. The anomaly's effects are just there as an excuse to have the format screw gimmick, and while there is a specific story in there, it doesn't fit with the point of the SCP formula nor use its strengths.

There's already plenty of SCPs that are little more than an excuse to break the format without any real meat to them, and like Jekeled said, ditching the scientific tone of the website doesn't make it special in any meaningful way.

-1, good writing, bad article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh okay, yeah I think you're right. It took me a while to wrap my head around this but I think I've got it… the fact that it's a semantic object, is part of the anomaly that it disrupts that separation of semantics between "categorization of anomaly" and "anomaly"? The description says that it's impossible to describe in significant detail and I wouldn't normally call the item number significantly detailed, so are you saying that the SCP is altering this?

It's also possible that the description itself is slightly inaccurate considering how this thing eats normality for breakfast. Now that I'm starting to see how much this thing messes with reality even in trying to conceptualize it in real life, I'm a bit more willing to accept this all. This is an anomaly that they have 100% given up on containing and I think I see why.

…+1 for being anomalous in real life…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. The prose itself is hard to follow, and I still don’t understand what the anomaly even is beyond a Lovecraftian-style force humanity has never and shouldn’t encounter. There’s been Lovecraftian-style SCPs and tales written before that have been much easier to follow. This is just… confusing. Beyond that, I can’t find myself coming up with a good enough reason for this piece to be formatted like this in a universe like the Foundation. Some entries have gotten away with breaking format for good reason, but this entry doesn’t have that good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disliking the prose is a perfectly valid criticism, but I'm… confused about the logic behind the number? Just because articles like SCP-3125 and SCP-055 have the designation semantically separate from the anomaly doesn't mean that'll apply to every article, especially one where the anomaly defies virtually every logical structure of our reality. Not all informational anomalies are going to follow the same behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered adding a crosslink at the "surreal" but decided against it — the more of a vacuum this article is in compared to the wider SCPverse the better, I feel. Greater disconnect from the SCPverse as a whole (which could offer some explanation to the anomaly) means greater alien and eldritch vibes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only real criticism of this is that I feel it should be connected to the Surrealistics Department

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed also, and I think the best example of this is the lack of an object number. From what I understand, the prose in place of clinical tone was a deliberate choice by the researcher in universe, because the object can't be explained in concrete terms. The item number would have no reason to be prose since the numbering occurs at an intrinsically separate semantic layer than the object itself. It's just an organization marker, and the number can be applied to things infinitely larger or more inexplicable. 3125 and 055 have numbers because understanding them and knowing what they are is a separate process from numbering them. etc

I personally find it interesting to have an object without concrete description, but I think there is a more clinical way to go about it. Having the clinical tone juxtaposed against flowery and intangible descriptions might be more in the SCP style, as right now it feels more like a wanderers library entry. Needs to be more grounded as an SCP article, and I think the Foundation is perfectly capable of doing that.

hope this comment is making sense / hope it makes sense as a reply rather than a new comment

edit: oh and novote, I think there are improvements that can be made that would at least push me personally to a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't want to directly state the meaning of it, but for rough implications:

+ spoilers?
- spoilers?
  • A lost child, searching for its mother.
  • Slideshows of blurred, indistinct childhood memories, smoldering with the embers of a faded warmth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While reading this, the only way I could make sense of this was by thinking: "I wonder if this is how algae on the surface of water react when someone decides to skip a stone across a lake?"

+1, largely because the note from the Head Staff puts this in an entirely different light. Not sure what it is, but it's significant somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is like 3999 but short

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Added "Sunrays, Muted" to the Esoteric Classes list.1 If you have a definition in mind, feel free to add it.

Footnotes
1. Unremarkably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this.

I'm getting a Sunless Sea/Sunless Skies/Fallen London etc. vibe from this, which I would normally see as positive, but this feels like more of a superficial foray into what those games do with less behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's difficult for me to see this as anything other than an excuse to throw a lot of prose around. The anomaly is almost beside the point; this style can be applied to so many things that it becomes the object of the article in itself, and imagery alone does not interest me. Downvoted.

E: I'd also like to add that one of the most interesting parts of the SCP format has always been the intersection of scientific tone and analysis with unscientific phenomena. Abandoning one of the items does not lead to interesting articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though I can't begin to explain why, I loved this one. There's a sort of quality about it which I loved reading, and even though I barely have a picture of it in my head, I feel like I understood everything about it which I was supposed to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was definitely getting into the Borges-style prose, but the identities section didn’t click for me and also broke the writing’s pacing and flow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There must be 20 or 30 scps at this point which are things that can't be described in a normal manner.

Toaster and 055 and the one that's just pictures are the only ones I find memorable. This missed the mark imho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's imagery I like here, especially nearing the end, but it's wrapped in something grating. Dropping clinical tone and justifying through the anomaly itself or the setting feels deeply unsatisfactory every time I see it. That might not be quite what I want to say, but I'm struggling to put it any better. I might just be looking for more framing than currently exists in the article to accompany the departure from standard tone. It feels jarring on its own, which might be the intended effect, but it didn't improve how I personally received it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This will probably be a very controversial skip that will annoy a lot of people, but I absolutely love this. Quite aside from my own love of stories told through rich imagery with initially obscure connections, which this pulls of perfectly, this has a wonderful sense of inevitability. So many skips try to describe the vast in concrete terms, using it to overawe the reader. This just accepts it, lets it flow, understands the meaningless of clinical tone so just lets it go.

Plus the prose is lovely and I'm very jealous of it :p +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I don't really get this but that doesn't mean I don't really love it

Update: I read this another like 10 times and I still don't really get it but I also still really like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really digging this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I kinda like what you're doing here, but its not compelling enough for me to upvote. Novote, I guess.

EDIT: Eh, good enough for an upvote.

item.title: tell me that you'll come back for me
item.content:encoded: Coldposted.
